stress\n- Deficient patients are susceptible to hemolytic anemia when exposed to oxidizing agents\n\n**CNSHA (Chronic Nonspherocytic Hemolytic Anemia):**\n\n\nCNSHA represents the most severe manifestation of G6PD deficiency, where Class I variants cause chronic hemolysis even without exposure to triggering agents, making these patients particularly vulnerable to additional oxidative stress.\n\n**Nitrofurantoin:**\nNitrofurantoin is an antibiotic used for urinary tract infections that generates reactive oxygen species, making it a known hemolytic trigger in G6PD-deficient patients through its oxidizing mechanism.\n\n**Clinical Implications for G6PD Deficient with CNSHA:**\nPatients with CNSHA face extreme risk from nitrofurantoin exposure since their baseline chronic hemolysis means any additional drug-induced oxidative stress could become life-threatening, so clinical guidelines recommend avoiding it entirely and using alternative antibiotics like trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, fluoroquinolones, or fosfomycin instead.\n\n**CPIC/Clinical Guidelines:**\nWhile CPIC doesn't have a standalone nitrofurantoin-G6PD guideline, the FDA label explicitly contraindicated nitrofurantoin in G6PD deficiency, and CPIC's G6PD guidance classifies it as a definite high-risk drug for hemolysis that should be avoided—making it contraindicated across all G6PD phenotypes, particularly in CNSHA where the consequences are most severe.\n\n**Recommendation:**\nAvoid nitrofurantoin entirely in G6PD-deficient patients; instead use alternatives like fosfomycin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole if susceptible, or fluoroquinolones. This is a strong, well-established contraindication because CNSHA represents the most severe form with markedly reduced or absent enzyme activity and chronic baseline hemolysis, putting patients at extreme risk for acute hemolytic crisis.\n\nFormatting the clinical response...","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":3,"cache_creation_input_tokens":5640,"cache_read_input_tokens":13930,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":5640,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":0,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"global"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"edcf0fe5-1c72-4946-8109-85f53878a8b8","uuid":"b386cad6-5777-4c98-a7eb-5bc91ab2201d"}

This drug is contraindicated in patients with G6PD deficiency, and especially in those with CNSHA (Class I severe deficiency). Select an alternative antibiotic for urinary tract infection treatment (e.g., fosfomycin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole if the organism is susceptible, or a fluoroquinolone based on culture data and clinical context).\",\n  \"classification\": \"Strong\",\n  \"implication\": \"G6PD deficiency with CNSHA represents the most severe phenotype (Class I), characterized by markedly reduced or absent G6PD enzyme activity and chronic baseline hemolysis even in the absence of exogenous triggers. Nitrofurantoin is an oxidizing nitrofuran compound that generates reactive oxygen species; in G6PD-deficient erythrocytes, the lack of sufficient NADPH production via the pentose phosphate pathway leaves red blood cells unable to neutralize oxidative stress, leading to glutathione depletion, hemoglobin denaturation (Heinz body formation), and acute hemolytic anemia. In a patient with pre-existing CNSHA, exposure to nitrofurantoin carries a very high risk of precipitating a severe acute hemolytic crisis superimposed on chronic hemolysis, potentially causing life-threatening anemia requiring transfusion. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","id":"msg_01CW6EXJ7QNxFBt7TpVwXPUg","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The file `/app/answers.json` has been written and verified. Here is a summary of the clinical reasoning:\n\n---\n\n**Drug-Gene Interaction: Nitrofurantoin + G6PD Deficiency (CNSHA)**\n\n**Classification: Strong**\n\n**Implication:**\nG6PD deficiency with CNSHA is a Class I (most severe) phenotype. These patients have chronically reduced/absent G6PD enzyme activity, leaving red blood cells unable to regenerate NADPH via the pentose phosphate pathway. Without adequate NADPH, glutathione cannot be maintained in its reduced form, making erythrocytes highly vulnerable to oxidative damage. These patients already suffer from chronic baseline hemolysis.\n\nNitrofurantoin is a nitrofuran antibiotic with well-characterized oxidizing properties. It generates reactive oxygen species that overwhelm the antioxidant defenses of G6PD-deficient RBCs, causing Heinz body formation and acute hemolytic anemia. In a patient with pre-existing CNSHA, this drug-induced oxidative insult can precipitate a severe acute hemolytic crisis on top of chronic hemolysis — potentially life-threatening.\n\n**Recommendation:**\nNitrofurantoin is **contraindicated**. This is reflected in the FDA labeling and aligns with CPIC/pharmacogenomic frameworks classifying nitrofurantoin as \"definite risk\" for hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients. Use an alternative agent (fosfomycin, TMP-SMX if susceptible, or a fluoroquinolone) guided by culture/sensitivity results."}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":527,"cache_read_input_tokens":21347,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":527,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"global"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"edcf0fe5-1c72-4946-8109-85f53878a8b8","uuid":"98b70c87-dc90-4bce-a39c-f67841d8f244"}
